SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its modeling assumptions and provides a complete operational description (Eqs. 1–7) and a training procedure (BPTS) including loss decomposition (Eq. 8). Experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines (PCFG for the synthetic task, SEQ2SEQ/SEQ2TREE and Quirk et al. baselines for IFTTT) and standard metrics (node/edge F1, production F1), though no statistical significance testing is reported. Some practical choices (teacher forcing, gold topo values during training, averaging gradients from multiple children) introduce potential exposure-bias or optimization subtleties that are acknowledged but not fully ablated, leaving minor gaps in understanding empirical robustness. Overall the technical presentation is consistent and the conclusions are supported by the experiments, with only minor missing analyses.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The doubly-recurrent cell that maintains separate ancestral and fraternal recurrences and explicitly predicts topology departs meaningfully from prior work (e.g. Zhang et al. 2016, Dong & Lapata 2016) by processing parent and sibling information with distinct modules and by predicting topology via sigmoids instead of special tokens (Section 3 and 3.1). The problem setting (fully generative tree decoding from an encoded vector) is known but underexplored, and the paper differentiates its contributions from related methods in Related Work. While the ideas are a non-trivial extension of recurrent architectures rather than a wholly new paradigm, they would be novel and informative to experts.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (intro, related work, method, experiments, discussion) and lists main contributions up front. Notation is mostly consistent and key computations are written explicitly (e.g. predictive state Eq. 3, topo probs Eqs. 5–6), and experimental details and dataset generation are provided in the appendix. A few implementation/training choices and ablation details are only briefly discussed (e.g. gradient averaging, exposure-bias remedies left to future work), and some appendix text is repetitive, but overall the presentation is clear and readable.
